title: binfmt_misc mountpoint missing in privileged containers
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

In order to be compatible with Docker, a privileged container should provide the binfmt_misc mountpoint.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. `sudo podman run --rm -it --privileged debian:buster-slim /bin/bash`

2. `mount | grep binfmt_misc`

**Describe the results you received:**

Empty output

**Describe the results you expected:**

```
binfmt_misc on /proc/sys/fs/binfmt_misc type binfmt_misc (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime)
```

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
(paste your output here)
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```
debug:
  compiler: gc
  git commit: ""
  go version: go1.13.5
  podman version: 1.7.0-rc1
host:
  BuildahVersion: 1.11.6
  CgroupVersion: v1
  Conmon:
    package: Unknown
    path: /nix/store/8xlzm0w62cd8qdpva0xg3ybnsm5ppkrp-conmon-2.0.8/bin/conmon
    version: 'conmon version 2.0.8, commit: unknown'
  Distribution:
    distribution: nixos
    version: 20.03.git.1bfdbc8
  MemFree: 9552855040
  MemTotal: 16640389120
  OCIRuntime:
    name: crun
    package: Unknown
    path: /run/current-system/sw/bin/crun
    version: |-
      crun version UNKNOWN
      spec: 1.0.0
      +SYSTEMD +SELINUX +APPARMOR +CAP +SECCOMP +EBPF +YAJL
  SwapFree: 0
  SwapTotal: 0
  arch: amd64
  cpus: 8
  eventlogger: journald
  hostname: demogorgon
  kernel: 5.4.3
  os: linux
  rootless: false
  uptime: 24m 20.43s
registries:
  blocked: null
  insecure: null
  search:
  - docker.io
  - registry.fedoraproject.org
  - registry.access.redhat.com
store:
  ConfigFile: /etc/containers/storage.conf
  ContainerStore:
    number: 3
  GraphDriverName: overlay
  GraphOptions: {}
  GraphRoot: /var/lib/containers/storage
  GraphStatus:
    Backing Filesystem: extfs
    Native Overlay Diff: "true"
    Supports d_type: "true"
    Using metacopy: "false"
  ImageStore:
    number: 56
  RunRoot: /var/run/containers/storage
  VolumePath: /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

NixOS

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

Physical

comments.body: Does Docker mount the /proc/sys from the host into the container?
Yes. Unfortunately, binfmt_misc is not yet namespace aware, although there are some [recent efforts](https://lkml.org/lkml/2019/11/7/577) to remedy this...
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@giuseppe Could you look into this one?

I've tried Docker 1.13 and I don't see binfmt mounted.  What version have you tried?

If it is not namespaces, I don't think we should add it by default.  Changes done there are visible to the host and it could be used to register malicious handlers.
I thought the problem was whether we do with --privileged flag?

> I thought the problem was whether we do with --privileged flag?

yes but I think it could still be a problem to add it by default since it is not namespaced.  Changes done to to the binfmt files will be propagated to the host and to every other container.  Do we want to do it by default?
No.  Does Docker do it by default?  Or only when used with --privileged?

Interestingly, I just realized that manually mounting binfmt_misc works using podman (in privileged mode, of course). So the problem seems to be with the container itself, as can be seen from these two commands:

```sh
$ sudo docker run --rm -it --privileged kasproject/kas-isar:1.1 mount | grep binfmt_misc
binfmt_misc on /proc/sys/fs/binfmt_misc type binfmt_misc (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime)
```

```sh
$ sudo podman run --rm -it --privileged kasproject/kas-isar:1.1 mount | grep binfmt_misc

```

While I tend to agree that setting up (or mounting) binfmt_misc from inside a container is not very clean, I still would expect the same behavior from docker and podman. (The mountpoint is required for cross-architecture builds.)

The container can be found on https://hub.docker.com/r/kasproject/kas-isar

> No. Does Docker do it by default? Or only when used with --privileged?

with Docker 1.13, I don't see it mounted in both cases

> > No. Does Docker do it by default? Or only when used with --privileged?
> 
> with Docker 1.13, I don't see it mounted in both cases

Maybe you should try a version of Docker from after the war :^)

@michaeladler, which version of Docker are you running?

Docker version 19.03.5, build 633a0ea838f10e000b7c6d6eed1623e6e988b5bc

However, please take notice of my last comment: https://github.com/containers/libpod/issues/4725#issuecomment-576268919 
It seems to be an issue with the way the specific container is built. Still, since the slogan of podman is `alias docker=podman`, I would expect this to work :)
@michaeladler What other mount points under /proc do you see?  My machine is currently running cgroups V2 so docker will not work.

I tried with Docker 18.06, which does not have the mount, but that's still 2 years old. I couldn't find direct mention in the release notes, so I'm not sure exactly when it landed.

An OCI spec from a privileged Docker container with the mount added would help a fair bit, so we know exactly what it's doing.

If I run `sudo mount binfmt_misc -t binfmt_misc /proc/sys/fs/binfmt_misc` inside the container, the mountpoint is there (as expected).

EDIT: I think there is something fishy going on. If I run another container image (e.g. debian:buster-slim), I do **not** see the binfmt_misc mountpoint in both Docker and podman. Thus the actual mount operation must happen somewhere in the entrypoint of the container (but there is no explicit mount statement). Maybe that mount fails with podman but works with Docker?

Ok, so I think I have nailed down this problem a little further:

1. Run debian:buster-slim in a privileged container, e.g. `docker run --privileged -it debian:buster-slim /bin/bash` (or podman instead of docker)
2. `apt-get update && apt-get install -y qemu-user-static`
3. `mount | grep binfmt`

If the container was created using docker, you will see the line `binfmt_misc on /proc/sys/fs/binfmt_misc type binfmt_misc (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime)`. In the case of podman, it is missing.

This can also be reproduced in an already running container:

1. `umount /proc/sys/fs/binfmt_misc`
2. `dpkg-reconfigure --force qemu-user-static`
3. `mount | grep binfmt`

In the case of docker, you will see `/proc/sys/fs/binfmt_misc mounted` again. In the case of podman, it is missing. **However, if I run `update-binfmts --enable` directly, it works in both docker and podman**. So the actual question is why does the package installation behave differently in Docker and podman.

Ok the difference seems to be your image is running the entrypoint of  --entrypoint=/kas/docker-entrypoint which is mounting or causing to mount the binfmt_misc mountpoint.

For some reason podman is not executing this or it is failing to mount.
@giuseppe I checked this against runc and crun and podman does them the same either way.

@rhatdan I have the same problem using the kas container.

docker:
```

```

```
total 0
dr-xr-xr-x 2 root root 0 Jul  8 16:51 .
dr-xr-xr-x 1 root root 0 Jul  8 16:51 ..
```

There is a "mount" call in the entry point, but that seems to be executed by docker as well as podman.
Any ideas?

```
$ podman --version
podman version 2.0.1
```

@fbezdeka You need to do the install, I belive.

The buster-slim image does not do `mount -t binfmt_misc binfmt_misc /proc/sys/fs/binfmt_misc ` automatically.

I will retest, but when running on docker it is getting mounted.

So we have different behaviour between docker and podman here.

If that is true, then Docker is mounting binfmt_misc by default when running --privileged, if that is true then that would be a significant difference that we should fix, however I don't believe it does.

Host: 
package `qemu-user-static` is installed, so `/proc/sys/fs/binfmt_misc` is "filled".

Both (docker and podman)
When starting the container `/proc/sys/fs/binfmt_misc` is empty.

Within container running on docker:
```
$ apt-get install qemu-user-static
...
$ ls /proc/sys/fs/binfmt_misc
(filled)
```

Within container running on podman:
```
$ apt-get install qemu-user-static
...
$ ls /proc/sys/fs/binfmt_misc
(stays empty)
```

When running on docker a kernel module named `binfmt_misc` gets loaded into the host kernel.

When running on podman this seems to fail (silently).

It is blocked by SELinux and might be blocked by non root user doing it.
Does it work if you disable SELinux for the container?
Does it work if you install it as rootfull podman.

SELinux:
It's not a SELinux problem. Even when running with a global "setenforce 0" the audit log keeps empty.

rootfull podman:
What exactly do you mean? I'm starting with sudo and the --privileged flag. Does that fully cover "rootfull podman"?

I just noticed that the mentioned `binfmt_misc` kernel module is already loaded during boot on the podman host.
So the installation of `qemu-user-static` fills `/proc/sys/fs/binfmt_misc` when running on docker, but it does not when running on podman.

As a workaround I can install `qemu-user-static` on the host and mount `/proc/sys/fs/binfmt_misc` manually inside the container. But I would like to avoid that.

I have no idea what in Podman would be blocking this mount.
I am trying to duplicate your error, and I see.
```
sudo podman run --privileged -it docker.io/library/debian:buster-slim bash
root@09b37fba3c34:/# apt-get install qemu-user-static
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree       
Reading state information... Done
E: Unable to locate package qemu-user-static
```

apt-get update 
fixes that issue.
Ok I don't see a problem in the install, but when it is done, the mount point does not exist.
Do you know what the post install script of qemu-user-static looks like?

It seems to work if I do --pid=host. 

So this looks like something different in the way we setup the /proc file system when running in Podman then in Docker.

Inside of Podman I see:
```
# mount | grep proc
proc on /proc type proc (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime)
```
Also could you make sure Docker uses PIDNS in privileged mode.
```
sudo podman run --privileged -it docker.io/library/debian:buster-slim ls -l /proc/self/ns/pid; ls -l /proc/self/ns/pid
lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 0 Jul  9 17:48 /proc/self/ns/pid -> 'pid:[4026534044]'
lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 0 Jul  9 13:48 /proc/self/ns/pid -> 'pid:[4026531836]'
```

PIDs on docker:
```
sudo docker run --privileged -it docker.io/library/debian:buster-slim ls -l /proc/self/ns/pid; ls -l /proc/self/ns/pid
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 0 Jul  9 18:18 /proc/self/ns/pid -> 'pid:[4026532267]'
lrwxrwxrwx 1 flo flo 0 Jul  9 20:18 /proc/self/ns/pid -> 'pid:[4026531836]'
```
The owner of the last one is different when comparing to podman, don't know if that matters.

@giuseppe PTAL,  I looked at this last summer, and the only thing I came up with is potentially docker and podman setting up pidns different?

this is not something we can fix I think.

The issue is in:

```
# head /var/lib/dpkg/info/qemu-user-static.postinst
#!/bin/sh
set -e
# check if we're running inside an (lxc) container
# (we may copy or move this to the postinst script too, to skip installing it)
grep -zqs ^container= /proc/1/environ && exit 0                              <= THIS IS THE ISSUE
```

so the `container=podman` environment variable blocks the postinst script to actually do the mount.

If I try:
```
# podman run --rm -it --privileged debian:buster-slim /bin/bash
# unset container
# exec bash
# apt-get update && apt-get install -y qemu-user-static
# mount | grep binfmt_misc
binfmt_misc on /proc/sys/fs/binfmt_misc type binfmt_misc (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime)
```
I get the mount.

This should be reported to Debian, although it seems to be on purpose and the issue is with Docker
Nice find @giuseppe
Could someone explain why `--pid=host` is fixing this problem then? I would be surprised if that touches the environment somehow...

That's not what I expected (and kind of weired), but `--pid=host` influences `/proc/1/environ`

Without `--pid=host` => container=podman
```
$ podman run --rm -it --privileged debian:buster-slim /bin/bash           
root@6fb226385718:/# grep -zs ^container= /proc/1/environ
container=podman
```

With `--pid=host` => container is unset
```
$ podman run --pid=host --rm -it --privileged debian:buster-slim /bin/bash 
root@b2616e2782ec:/# grep -zs ^container= /proc/1/environ
root@b2616e2782ec:/#
```

Anyway, I will try to open a bug for the Debian qemu-user-static package.
